the gap between knowing a tool exists and actually integrating it into your workflow is where most devs lose
this would've saved me 3 hours last week. bookmarking
the best developer advice is always unsexy. fundamentals > shiny new frameworks
automation isn't about being lazy. it's about being consistent at scale
shipping > perfection. every time. the best code is the code that's actually in production
the 10x engineer myth is dead. 10x teams are real though. and they share stuff like this
I've seen teams spend more time debating the approach than it would take to just try both. bias toward action
this is why I prefer working with people who've maintained systems, not just built them. maintenance teaches you what actually matters
ai: AI doesn't replace thinking. it accelerates the output of people who already think clearly
the people getting the most out of AI tools are the ones who know exactly what they want before they prompt
context engineering is the real skill. the prompt is just the last mile
most people use AI at maybe 20% of its capability. the unlock is treating it as a collaborator, not a search engine
the gap between 'I use ChatGPT' and 'I use AI in my workflow' is enormous. it's about systems, not individual prompts
the best AI workflow is one where the human does the thinking and the AI does the typing
prompt engineering tip: constraints > instructions. tell the model what NOT to do and quality doubles
AI is a force multiplier. 2x engineer + AI = 10x output. 0.5x engineer + AI = 1x output with hallucinations
finance: the market is pricing in the worst case scenario. historically, that's been a decent entry signal
